title: AI advice made people three times less accurate but twice as confident, researchers found
blurb: Researchers from three French and Italian universities found that access to AI advice cut accuracy from 27% to 9% while confidence rose from 30% to 76%, and willingness to say "I don't know" collapsed from 44% to 3%. The study deliberately used questions the model got wrong, so the drop cannot be explained as sensible delegation, and monetary incentives recovered only a few points.

title: As many companies cut AI costs by using cheaper models, some, like Shopify, go all-in on frontier models, barring engineers from using anything else (Belle Lin/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Shopify bars its engineers from using anything but frontier models, running counter to a broader push to cut AI spend by routing work to cheaper, smaller models. The bet is that engineer time costs more than inference, so the gap between a top model and a budget one shows up in output quality rather than the cloud bill.

title: The CEO Trying to Fix PayPal Has a New Option: Sell It for Billions
blurb: PayPal received a buyout offer from one of its payments rivals, giving the company a path out of its turnaround rather than through it. The CEO had been pushing an ambitious plan to revive growth, and a multibillion-dollar sale now competes with that plan for the board's attention.

title: As many companies cut AI costs by using cheaper models, some, like Shopify, go all-in on frontier models, barring engineers from using anything else (Belle Lin/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: As enterprises cut AI spending by routing work to cheaper models, Shopify has taken the opposite approach and requires its engineers to use frontier models only. The company argues that the productivity gain from top-tier models outweighs the per-token cost, a bet that runs against the broader corporate shift toward cost-optimized model routing.

title: AI advice made people three times less accurate but twice as confident, researchers found
blurb: Researchers from three French and Italian universities found that access to AI advice cut participants' accuracy from 27% to 9% while their confidence rose from 30% to 76%. Willingness to say "I don't know" collapsed from 44% to 3%, and monetary incentives recovered only part of the gap, lifting accuracy to 16%. The study deliberately used questions the model got wrong, so the drop cannot be explained as sensible delegation to a reliable tool.

title: This experiment shows how easy it is to poison an open-weight AI model for under $100
blurb: Cybersecurity researcher Katie Paxton-Fear backdoored an open-weight model with ten poisoned training examples, spending under $100 and about an hour to make it reliably emit code vulnerable to remote code execution. Larger models proved easier to compromise than smaller ones, and there is no reliable way to detect a manipulated model, since open weights offer nowhere near the transparency of reverse-engineerable software.

title: Etzioni on AI: Uncle Sam wants a stake in leading AI companies — what could possibly go wrong?
blurb: Sanders and Trump have both endorsed public ownership of a slice of the AI industry, though they disagree on the size of the stake and whether the equity is donated or seized. The author argues the difference between a dividend and a trap comes down to three unenforced conditions: a small stake, a wall between the government as owner and as regulator, and proceeds earmarked for displaced workers rather than the general fund.

title: I burned all my tokens researching how to save tokens
blurb: This post documents how a developer rebuilt a deep research pipeline after the first version exhausted a Claude Max 5x limit in 30 minutes, launching 111 agents and verifying only 25 of 123 queued claims before the synthesis step never ran. The rewrite spreads work across Claude, Codex, and Antigravity subscriptions with a shared memory layer via an extended claude-mem plugin, and routes subagent tasks to cheaper models selected on Terminal-Bench and SWE-bench Pro results.

title: Moonshot AI suspends new subscriptions due to Kimi K3 demand
blurb: Moonshot AI paused new Kimi subscriptions after K3 demand hit GPU capacity limits, and will split membership into separate Kimi and Kimi Code plans.

title: As many companies cut AI costs by using cheaper models, some, like Shopify, go all-in on frontier models, barring engineers from using anything else (Belle Lin/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: The AI cost-optimization consensus is that you route cheap and reserve frontier models for the hard cases, but Shopify went the other direction and barred its engineers from using anything but frontier models. The bet is that engineer time costs more than tokens, and downgrading the model quietly degrades output in ways nobody catches until it ships. Both strategies can be right, and the split depends on whether your AI spend sits in the product, where unit economics rule, or in internal engineering, where throughput does.

title: Etzioni on AI: Uncle Sam wants a stake in leading AI companies — what could possibly go wrong?
blurb: Sanders and Trump agree on almost nothing, but both want the public to own a piece of the AI industry, and OpenAI and Anthropic have each backed a version of the idea. The precedent is already set: Treasury paid $8.9B for 9.9% of Intel in August 2025, a stake worth roughly $36B by the following spring, and the Pentagon has since taken 15% of a rare-earth miner. The AI proposals differ in one key way, since the equity would be donated rather than bought, which costs taxpayers nothing up front but makes the government both owner and referee of the industry it regulates.

title: Jason’s Takes on This Week’s 20VC: The Token Governor, the Net-New-Logo Test, and Why Renewal Stopped Being Safe
blurb: ClickHouse's AI spend is up 60x since February, and without a spend governor the incentive structure guarantees it keeps climbing because any individual can spin up a bill that accrues to the company, not to them. Cost per token is the wrong metric to manage it with, since a model with cheap base tokens can burn unpredictable volumes of reasoning tokens and lose on cost per completed task. Different models win different jobs, so the job is managing a portfolio and putting a governor on the line item before your CFO does.

title: Can an Apple lawsuit derail OpenAI’s hardware plans?
blurb: Apple filed a trade secrets lawsuit against OpenAI, alleging a pattern of recruiting current and former Apple employees for confidential information. OpenAI says it is not aware of any evidence the complaint has merit, but the timing lands on the hardware division it is building with Jony Ive, starting with a mobile smart speaker, and on its path to going public. Litigation slows product timelines whether or not a court grants relief, which is often the point of filing. The open question is whether OpenAI settles fast or, after beating Elon Musk in court, decides it can absorb a trial.

title: The CEO Trying to Fix PayPal Has a New Option: Sell It for Billions
blurb: PayPal's turnaround plan now has a rival buyout offer sitting next to it, and the CEO has to defend one against the other. A bid forces the board to price a multiyear execution story against cash today, which is a fight turnarounds usually lose. Once a category leader's growth depends on execution three years out, acquirers get to argue the discount is permanent, and the plan stops being a strategy and becomes a negotiating position.

title: Harness Engineering
blurb: The author argues that agent output improves most by treating the model and coding agent as a fixed black box and engineering the two external levers instead: context and tools. The repository frames this as last-mile work of encoding an organization's nonfunctional requirements, operational state, and authority relationships into retrievable context, examples, and executable checks, on the premise that general model weights never contain private process data.

title: I burned all my tokens researching how to save tokens
blurb: The author's first deep research run burned an entire Claude Max 5x limit in 30 minutes, launching 111 agents and verifying only 25 of 123 queued claims before the synthesis stage ever ran. The rebuild extends the claude-mem plugin to share memory across Claude Code, Codex, and Antigravity, and routes subagent work to cheaper models picked from Terminal-Bench and SWE-bench Pro results.

title: This experiment shows how easy it is to poison an open-weight AI model for under $100
blurb: Researcher Katie Paxton-Fear backdoored an open-weight model with ten poisoned fine-tuning examples, making it reliably emit RCE-vulnerable code, at a cost of under $100 and roughly an hour of work. Larger models proved easier to compromise than smaller ones, and there is no reliable way to detect a poisoned model, so teams pulling open weights should treat provenance and fine-tune supply chain as controls, not assumptions.

title: SonicWall SMA Zero-Days Exploited Before Disclosure to Gain Root Access
blurb: Volexity attributed exploitation of two SonicWall SMA 1000 zero-days, CVE-2026-15409 (CVSS 10.0) and CVE-2026-15410 (CVSS 7.2), to a new threat actor tracked as UTA0533, which chained them for root command execution starting June 22. The actor deployed a setuid ROOTRUN binary, the KNUCKLEBALL Python loader, Suo5, and an ORANGETAIL web shell, plus tcpdump capture of cleartext LDAP credentials. SonicWall patched both this week.

title: UAC-0145 Uses ClickFix CAPTCHAs to Infect Ukrainian Devices wih Malware
blurb: CERT-UA attributed a ClickFix campaign to UAC-0145, a Sandworm sub-cluster tied to Russia's GRU, that compromised at least 10 websites between June and July 2026 to serve fake CAPTCHAs prompting victims to paste PowerShell into their terminals. The chain drops GHETTOVIBE, SCOUTCURL recon, and the FREAKYPOLL Python backdoor, uses EtherHiding to resolve C2 domains from an Ethereum smart contract, and pairs with COWARDDUCK Android APKs disguised as security tools.

title: Hackers abuse ViPNet software to target Russian govt agencies
blurb: Kaspersky documented HelloNet, a campaign active since at least May that sideloads a malicious wtsapi32.dll into the ViPNet Update System directory, injecting a proxy loader into svchost.exe for elevated privileges and reboot persistence across Russian government, energy, transport, and logistics targets. ViPNet's update infrastructure itself was not compromised, so defenders should audit update directories for unsigned DLLs and treat gaps in ViPNet logs as evidence of the HelloCleaner module.

title: SonicWall SMA Zero-Days Exploited Before Disclosure to Gain Root Access
blurb: Volexity attributed exploitation of two SonicWall SMA 1000 VPN zero-days, CVE-2026-15409 (CVSS 10.0) and CVE-2026-15410, to a new threat actor tracked as UTA0533, with activity dating back to June 22 and patches only released this week. The chain delivered a setuid root binary, a Java web shell, and tcpdump-based harvesting of plaintext LDAP credentials, so patching alone leaves compromised appliances and stolen directory credentials in place.

title: Alarmas que despiertan por causa real, no por un número random
blurb: An AWS Builders post argues that alarms on output counts page on coincidence, since `count == 0` cannot distinguish a broken pipeline from a correctly quiet night. The author's fix keeps the threshold unchanged and moves the intelligence to the emitter, using a pure classifier that labels why the count was zero and emits a separate unalarmed metric for benign runs. A companion repo walks through the diagnosis SQL, the instrumented job, and tests that cover the classification rather than the plumbing.

title: TBM 431: The Denominator That Matters
blurb: John Cutler proposes a multiplicative model for AI adoption: machine understanding times problem understanding times practice evolution, all over a denominator of organizational social contract. Any term at zero kills the product, so a team with deep domain expertise but no mental model of how the tools fail iterates fast in the wrong direction. His sharpest point is that resistance to AI mandates is rational rather than emotional, since improvement gains have no credible guarantee of not being captured against the people who generated them.

title: The GENIUS Act turns 1: State of Crypto
blurb: The GENIUS Act passed its one-year mark with federal stablecoin rules still unfinalized, as the OCC, FDIC, and other agencies work through proposals covering reserves, custody, capital, and KYC requirements for issuers. The FDIC alone published 144 questions on how it would supervise issuers, and final rules are expected within months while the industry pushes for the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act.

title: Kalshi and Polymarket bets on the FIFA World Cup final top $5.69B; Dune Analytics says total wagers on the platforms surpassed $50B for the first time in June (Lauren McCarthy/New York Times)
blurb: Kalshi and Polymarket took a combined $5.69B in bets on the FIFA World Cup final, the largest single-event volume either platform has recorded. Dune Analytics data shows total wagers across the two venues passed $50B for the first time in June, putting regulated prediction markets on a volume footing comparable to mid-tier centralized exchanges.

title: The GENIUS Act turns 1: State of Crypto
blurb: The GENIUS Act hit its one-year anniversary with federal stablecoin rules still unfinished, as the OCC and FDIC work through proposals covering reserves, custody, capital and liquidity standards. The FDIC has published 144 questions on issuer oversight and the OCC issued its own interpretation in February, with final rules expected within months and KYC requirements for issuers likely to mirror those on traditional financial firms.

title: The CEO Trying to Fix PayPal Has a New Option: Sell It for Billions
blurb:
PayPal received a multibillion-dollar buyout offer from a rival, giving CEO Enrique Lores an exit path from a turnaround plan that has yet to show results. A sale would consolidate one of the largest consumer wallets and merchant-acquiring books in payments, putting checkout, Venmo, and Braintree under a competitor's control.

51 words, 2 sentences.

Flag: the context names Enrique Lores as PayPal's CEO, but he runs HP. Confirm against the WSJ piece before publishing.
